title: Passive reactivity control of nuclear thermal propulsion reactors
abstract: Passive reactivity control technologies that enable reactivity control of a nuclear thermal propulsion (NTP) system with little to no active mechanical movement of circumferential control drums. By minimizing or eliminating the need for mechanical movement of the circumferential control drums during an NTP burn, the reactivity control technologies simplify controlling an NTP reactor and increase the overall performance of the NTP system. The reactivity control technologies mitigate and counteract the effects of xenon, the dominant fission product contributing to reactivity transients. Examples of reactivity control technologies include, employing burnable neutron poisons, tuning hydrogen pressure, adjusting wait time between burn cycles or merging burn cycles, and enhancement of temperature feedback mechanisms. The reactivity control technologies are applicable to low-enriched uranium NTP systems, including graphite composite fueled and tungsten ceramic and metal matrix (CERMET), or any moderated NTP system, such as highly- enriched uranium graphite composite NTP systems.
claims:
Claims 38  What is Claimed Is 1. A nuclear thermal propulsion system comprising: a nuclear reactor core including: an array of fuel elements; an array of tie tubes adjacent the array of fuel elements, each tie tube including: a propellant supply passage to flow a propellant; an inner tie tube layer surrounding the propellant supply passage; a moderator sleeve surrounding the inner tie tube layer; a propellant return passage surrounding the moderator sleeve; and an outer tie tube layer surrounding the propellant return passage; and a burnable poison dispersed in the nuclear reactor core. 2. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 1, wherein the burnable poison is dispersed in the array of fuel elements or the array of tie tubes. 3. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 2, wherein: the burnable poison includes Gadolinium (Gd); the Gd is dispersed in an alloy that forms the outer tie tube layer; and the propellant is hydrogen. 4. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 3, wherein the Gd dispersed in the outer tie tube layer alloy is in a quantity greater than 0 parts per million (ppm) and less than one-thousand (1 ,000) ppm. 5. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 4, wherein the Gd in the outer tie tube layer alloy is a natural isotopic composition or an enriched l 57 Gd isotope. 6. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 5, wherein the outer tie tube layer alloy further includes nickel, chromium, and iron. 7. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 6, wherein the Gd dispersed in the outer tie tube layer alloy further includes molybdenum, niobium, cobalt, manganese, copper, aluminum, titanium, silicon, carbon, sulfur, phosphorus, and boron. 8. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 6, wherein the outer tie tube layer alloy is also formed of zirconium or zirconium carbide. 9. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 3, wherein: the array of fuel elements includes a graphite composite fuel formed of low-enriched uranium (LEU) having 20% or lower 235 U; and 

 39 the moderator sleeve includes a solid hydride. 10. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 9, wherein the Gd dispersed in the outer tie tube layer alloy is in a quantity of 20 parts per million (ppm) to 200 ppm. 1 1. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 3, wherein: the array of fuel elements includes a tungsten ceramic and metal matrix (CERMET) fuel formed of low-enriched uranium (LEU) having 20% or lower 235 U ; and the moderator sleeve includes a solid hydride. 12. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 3 : wherein each tie tube further comprises: an inner gap between the inner tie tube layer and the moderator sleeve; a graphite insulation layer surrounding the outer tie tube layer; a medial gap between the outer tie tube layer and the graphite insulation layer; an outer gap surrounding the zirconium carbide layer; a graphite sleeve surrounding the outer gap; and a coating formed of zirconium carbide or niobium carbide surrounding the graphite sleeve. 13. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 12, wherein the graphite insulation layer includes zirconium carbide (ZrC), titanium carbide, silicon carbide, tantalum carbide, hafnium carbide, ZrC-ZrB 2 composite, or ZrC-ZrB 2 -SiC composite. 14. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 3, wherein: the propellant supply passage, the inner tie tube layer, the moderator sleeve, the propellant return passage, and the outer tie tube layer are radially arranged; and a respective radial wall thickness of the outer tie tube layer is less than the inner tie tube layer. 15. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 14, wherein the array of fuel elements are interspersed with the array of tie tubes and each of the tie tubes is in direct or indirect contact with at least one fuel element. 16. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 14, further comprising: a plurality of circumferential control drums surrounding the array of fuel elements and the array of tie tubes to change reactivity of the nuclear reactor core. 

 40 17. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 2, wherein the burnable poison includes Gadolinium (Gd) and the Gd is dispersed in an alloy that forms the inner tie tube layer. 18. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 2, wherein the burnable poison includes Gadolinium (Gd) and the Gd is dispersed in each of the fuel elements. 19. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 2, wherein: the moderator sleeve is formed of zirconium hydride; the burnable poison includes Gadolinium (Gd); and the Gd is dispersed in the zirconium hydride that forms the moderator sleeve. 20. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 1 , wherein the nuclear reactor core further comprises: at least one wire formed of an alloy that is housed inside the nuclear reactor core and separate from the array of fuel elements and the array of tie tubes; the burnable poison includes Gadolinium (Gd); and the Gd is dispersed in the at least one alloy wire. 21. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 1 , further comprising: a propeilant tank to store the propeilant; a nuclear reactor core inlet directly or indirectly connected to the nuclear reactor core; a propeilant line directly or indirectly connected to the propeilant tank to flow the propeilant to the nuclear reactor core inlet; and a propeilant density control valve system directly or indirectly connected between the propeilant line and the nuclear reactor core inlet to regulate density of the propeilant flowing into the nuclear reactor core. 22. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 21 , further comprising: a turbopump assembly comprising at least one turbopump that includes a turbine and a pump; and wherein the pump is configured to cool the nuclear reactor core during a burn cycle by flowing the propeilant from the propeilant tank through the propeilant line to the propeilant density control valve system, and then through the nuclear reactor core inlet. 23. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 22, wherein: 

 41 the nuclear reactor core inlet includes a tie tube inlet to flow the propellant to the array of tie tubes and is directly or indirectly connected to the propellant density control valve system and a respective propellant supply passage of a respective tie tube in the array of tie tubes; and the pump is further configured to flow the propellant through the tie tube inlet, through the respective propellant supply passage of the respective tie tube in the array of tie tubes, and then through a respective propellant return passage of the respective tie tube. 24. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 23, further comprising: a propellant heating line directly or indirectly connected to the turbine and the respective propellant return passage of the respective tie tube; a second propellant density control valve system directly or indirectly connected to the turbine and the nuclear reactor core inlet to regulate density of the propellant flowing back to the nuclear reactor core inlet; wherein the pump is configured to flow the propellant returned by the respective propellant return passage to the turbine via the propellant heating line; and wherein the turbine is configured to flow the propellant back to the second propellant density control valve system and the nuclear reactor core inlet. 25. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 24, wherein: the nuclear reactor core inlet further includes a fuel element inlet to flow the propellant to the array of fuel elements and is directly or indirectly connected to the second propellant density control valve system and a respective propellant passage of a respective fuel element of the array of fuel elements; and the turbine is configured to flow the propellant to the second propellant density control valve system, through the fuel element inlet, and then through the respective propellant passage of the respective fuel element of the array of fuel elements. 26. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 22, further comprising: a pressure vessel housing the nuclear reactor core that is directly or indirectly connected to the propellant density control valve system and including: a pressure vessel inlet connected to the periphery of the pressure vessel that is outside of the nuclear reactor core; and 

 42 a pressure vessel outlet to return the propellant passing through the periphery of the pressure vessel; a propellant heating line directly or indirectly connected to the turbine and the pressure vessel outlet; and wherein the pump is further configured to flow the propellant to the pressure vessel inlet, through the periphery of the pressure vessel, and then flow the returned propellant from the pressure vessel outlet to the turbine via the propellant heating line. 27. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 26, further comprising: a nozzle; a plurality of control drums; a neutron reflector; and a second propellant density control valve system directly or indirectly connected between the propellant line and the pressure vessel inlet to regulate density of the propellant flowing into the nozzle, the periphery of the pressure vessel, the neutron reflector, and the plurality of control drums. 28. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 21 , wherein the propellant density control valve system includes: a regulator valve to adjust density of the propellant passing through the regulator valve from an initial density when entering the regulator valve to a regulated density when exiting the regulator valve; and an actuator to actuate the regulator valve by adjusting the regulated propellant density upwards during each subsequent burn cycle to maintain constant reactivity at a beginning of each of the subsequent burn cycles. 29. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 28, wherein the actuator is electric, mechanical, thermal, magnetic, or a combination thereof. 30. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 29, wherein the propellant density control valve system further includes a flow control circuit to control speed of the actuator and the flow control circuit is a bleed-off circuit, a meter-out circuit, or a meter-in circuit. 31. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 28, wherein the regulator valve and the actuator form a solenoid valve or an electrohydraulic servovalve. 

 43 32. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 31 , wherein the regulator valve and the actuator form the solenoid valve and the solenoid valve is controlled by electric signals conveyed from an external computer, a digital circuit, an analog circuit, or a combination thereof. 33. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 28, wherein: the propellant is hydrogen; and the regulator valve is configured to adjust density of the hydrogen from the initial density to the regulated density by varying pressure between 100% and 200% of nominal hydrogen pressure, inclusive, to increase reactivity of the nuclear reactor core. 34. The nuclear thermal propulsion system of claim 33, wherein the nominal hydrogen pressure is 5 to 15 megapascals (MPa).
